ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper adapts an established SICK-building procedure to Polish and produces a sizable 10K-pair corpus, which is useful but largely an instantiation rather than a fundamentally new idea. The introduction of language-specific dependency-based transformation rules and a two-stage human verification workflow represents a non-trivial, language-tailored engineering effort, but it reads as an adaptation of existing methods rather than a novel method or theoretical perspective. Given the lack of new formalization of the target compositional phenomena or methodological departures from prior SICK-style pipelines, the contribution is incremental and would be unsurprising to experts in corpus construction for CDS. | B(3): The paper produces a new, sizable Polish counterpart to SICK and introduces language-specific dependency-based transformation rules plus a practical two-stage annotation and leader verification workflow — a useful and pragmatic resource contribution. However, the core idea (adapting SICK-style construction and image-seeded transformations) is an incremental application of existing procedures rather than a fundamentally new method. From my perspective as a resource-focused reviewer, the combination of automatic transformation + human correction and the scale (10K pairs) is valuable, but the technical novelty beyond prior SICK-style pipelines is moderate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper adapts an established SICK-building procedure to Polish and produces a sizable 10K-pair corpus, which is useful but largely an instantiation rather than a fundamentally new idea. The introduction of language-specific dependency-based transformation rules and a two-stage human verification workflow represents a non-trivial, language-tailored engineering effort, but it reads as an adaptation of existing methods rather than a novel method or theoretical perspective. Given the lack of new formalization of the target compositional phenomena or methodological departures from prior SICK-style pipelines, the contribution is incremental and would be unsurprising to experts in corpus construction for CDS.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper produces a new, sizable Polish counterpart to SICK and introduces language-specific dependency-based transformation rules plus a practical two-stage annotation and leader verification workflow — a useful and pragmatic resource contribution. However, the core idea (adapting SICK-style construction and image-seeded transformations) is an incremental application of existing procedures rather than a fundamentally new method. From my perspective as a resource-focused reviewer, the combination of automatic transformation + human correction and the scale (10K pairs) is valuable, but the technical novelty beyond prior SICK-style pipelines is moderate.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with the adapted SICK-building pipeline laid out (seed selection, dependency-based transformations, pairing scheme, two-stage annotation) and the main contributions (10K Polish sentence-pair corpus, language-specific transformation rules, verification workflow) presented early. Inter-annotator agreement is reported with appropriate statistics (Fleiss' κ = 0.732, Krippendorff's α = 0.78), which strengthens clarity about data quality. The summary does not mention the paper’s formal notation or whether figures/tables and examples are systematically used, so while the writing quality is reported as good and the procedural description is clear, a few presentation elements (explicit notation definitions and visibility of illustrative figures/tables) are uncertain from the provided summary. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and states its main contributions early: a procedure for building a Polish CDS evaluation set, language-specific transformation rules, and a two-stage annotation workflow. Writing quality is reported as good and evaluation/annotation metrics are clearly presented (Fleiss’ κ, Krippendorff’s α). Minor presentation gaps remain in the summary: notation/detailing of formal definitions isn’t mentioned and figures/tables/examples are not explicitly reported, so a small number of clarifying edits or added illustrative material would make the presentation exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with the adapted SICK-building pipeline laid out (seed selection, dependency-based transformations, pairing scheme, two-stage annotation) and the main contributions (10K Polish sentence-pair corpus, language-specific transformation rules, verification workflow) presented early. Inter-annotator agreement is reported with appropriate statistics (Fleiss' κ = 0.732, Krippendorff's α = 0.78), which strengthens clarity about data quality. The summary does not mention the paper’s formal notation or whether figures/tables and examples are systematically used, so while the writing quality is reported as good and the procedural description is clear, a few presentation elements (explicit notation definitions and visibility of illustrative figures/tables) are uncertain from the provided summary.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and states its main contributions early: a procedure for building a Polish CDS evaluation set, language-specific transformation rules, and a two-stage annotation workflow. Writing quality is reported as good and evaluation/annotation metrics are clearly presented (Fleiss’ κ, Krippendorff’s α). Minor presentation gaps remain in the summary: notation/detailing of formal definitions isn’t mentioned and figures/tables/examples are not explicitly reported, so a small number of clarifying edits or added illustrative material would make the presentation exceptional.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the manuscript lacks any empirical baselines or head-to-head comparisons, which prevents assessing how existing CDS or semantic models perform on the new dataset. Because meaningful experimental comparison is essential to demonstrate the dataset’s impact and the paper provides only documentation without model evaluations, the work fails to meet the standard for comparative contribution and thus receives the lowest score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a dataset construction procedure but includes no empirical comparisons to any baselines or prior CDS models (summary states "Baselines compared: none mentioned"). As a theory-focused reviewer I penalize the absence of head-to-head evaluations and fair experimental conditions: there is no way to judge whether the resource yields different model behavior or improves over existing benchmarks. While related work coverage is reported as adequate, citation alone does not substitute for demonstrations that prior methods perform comparably or worse, nor for analysis of differences between approaches.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a substantial dataset and adapts SICK-style procedures to Polish, but it does not include any empirical baselines or model comparisons—‘Baselines compared: none mentioned’—so it fails to evaluate how existing CDS or semantic models perform on the new benchmark. While related work is discussed adequately and the dataset methodology is well documented, the absence of experiments comparing prior methods or acknowledging when they match or exceed proposed outcomes makes the comparisons inadequate for assessing contribution significance to the community.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers concur that the paper delivers its primary contribution—a 10K-pair Polish CDS-style corpus with sound annotation methodology and solid inter-annotator agreement—and that the dataset construction is reasonable despite language-specific challenges. The main weaknesses are the lack of downstream evaluations, transformation-failure statistics, and explicit release/reproducibility artifacts; these are important but not fatal to the technical contribution, so a slightly above-midrange score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states and largely supports its primary claim of producing a Polish CDS evaluation corpus (10K pairs) with respectable annotation reliability (Fleiss’ κ=0.732, Krippendorff’s α=0.78). However, from a principled compositionality-evaluation perspective it omits crucial evidence: no extrinsic downstream evaluations (≥2 tasks) demonstrating that the resource changes model behavior, and no per-rule error analysis quantifying failures of the language-specific automatic transformation pipeline (only human correction is reported). Methodologically the corpus construction and two-stage annotation are reasonable, but the absence of transformation failure statistics and downstream utility experiments leaves important claims about the dataset’s practical value under-supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states and supports its main claims: it describes an adapted SICK-style process for Polish, reports creation of a 10K-pair corpus with a two-stage human verification pipeline, and provides inter-annotator agreement (κ = 0.732, α = 0.78), which substantiates dataset quality. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental methodology for dataset construction and annotation is sound and pragmatic given language-specific parsing limitations; human correction mitigates parser errors. Minor weaknesses affect reproducibility and completeness (no code or explicit public release mentioned, and no downstream baselines or benchmarking to demonstrate dataset utility), but these do not undermine the main technical contribution.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper produces a valuable Polish CDS resource with solid annotation practice and agreement metrics, and the two-stage workflow supports quality and reproducibility. However, they also note missing elements—formal theoretical framing, per-rule error analysis, baseline/extrinsic evaluations, and explicit release/code information—that limit its completeness for a top-tier submission. Given the clear practical contribution but these gaps, a score of 4 reflects it as a strong, useful paper that would benefit from addressing those weaknesses.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a relevant NLP topic (Polish CDS evaluation data) and follows rigorous annotation practice (Fleiss' κ = 0.732, Krippendorff's α = 0.78) which makes it suitable for the community, but from a Theory Purist perspective it is primarily a dataset construction effort without a formalization of the target compositional phenomena or any downstream utility demonstrations. The methodology relies on language-specific automatic transformation rules and imperfect dependency parsing yet provides no per-rule error analysis or quantified transformation failure modes, and no baselines or ≥2 extrinsic evaluations are reported to show how the resource changes model behaviour. Given these strengths and shortcomings, the submission is borderline appropriate: valuable as a resource but lacking the theoretical framing and evaluation breadth I would expect for a top-tier conference paper.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a good fit for a top NLP venue: it addresses a practical community need by creating a sizable (10K-pair) Polish CDS testset adapted from the well-known SICK protocol, uses a sensible mix of automatic dependency-based transformations plus human verification, and reports appropriate agreement metrics (Fleiss’ κ = 0.732, Krippendorff’s α = 0.78). The two-stage annotation workflow and leader verification align with reproducibility and quality expectations I value, but missing details such as public code/release mention and absence of baseline model evaluations slightly weaken readiness for the main conference track, making it a strong but not perfect fit.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper fills an important resource gap for Polish compositional semantics and provides careful annotation and transformation procedures, but its impact is limited by being primarily a dataset engineering effort with no downstream evaluations and unclear portability/code release. Given these strengths and the noted limitations, a middle-ground score reflects that the corpus will be useful and adopted within the Polish NLP community but is unlikely to be broadly transformative without additional evaluations or release details.

reviewer_a.justification: Creating a Polish SICK-like corpus of 10K sentence pairs with solid annotation agreement (Fleiss’ κ=0.732, Krippendorff’s α=0.78) addresses a real resource gap for compositional distributional semantics, but the contribution is primarily a dataset engineering effort rather than a theoretical advance. The paper lacks any downstream evaluations or baselines (no ≥2 extrinsic tasks) to demonstrate how the corpus changes model behavior, and it relies on language-specific automatic transformation rules without reported per-rule error analysis — both of which limit the work’s broader impact. Given these omissions, the resource is useful but likely to have limited, niche influence rather than shift how researchers think about CDS.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important gap by producing a large (10K-pair) Polish SICK-style evaluation set with language-specific transformation rules and a two-stage human verification pipeline, which aligns with best practices I value for dataset work. The careful annotation (κ=0.732, α=0.78) and use of automatic dependency-based transformations plus human correction make it a practical resource that is likely to be adopted by Polish CDS and semantic similarity researchers. Lack of mentioned code release and dependence on imperfect parsing limit immediate portability to other languages, so while this will strongly impact Polish NLP and enable new work, it is not fully transformative beyond the language without further release/replication support.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers a substantial, well-annotated Polish CDS dataset with strong inter-annotator agreement and careful practical discussion, but they also concur it lacks key empirical validations (downstream task evaluations, model baselines), ablation/sensitivity and per-rule error analyses, and does not ensure reproducibility via code release. Given the clear value of the resource but the missing analyses and release details that limit its scientific impact and validation, a middle-ground score reflects a useful contribution that requires further work to be fully convincing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper produces a sizable Polish CDS test set and reports solid annotation reliability (Fleiss’ κ=0.732, Krippendorff’s α=0.78) and discusses practical limitations (parser errors, language-specific transformations). However, it lacks the extrinsic downstream evaluations I require (no ≥2 downstream tasks or model comparisons), contains no ablation/sensitivity analyses of the transformation pipeline, and offers no per-rule error analysis despite relying on automatic transformation rules. Related work is reported as adequate, but reproducibility is weakened by no code release or detailed transformation/failure statistics.

reviewer_b.justification: This work presents a substantial, pragmatic resource-building effort (10K Polish sentence pairs, language-specific dependency-based transformation rules, and a two-stage annotation workflow with leader verification) and reports solid inter-annotator agreement (Fleiss' κ=0.732, Krippendorff α=0.78), which supports its main claims. However, the paper lacks model baselines, ablation or sensitivity analyses of the transformation/verification pipeline, and code availability is not mentioned — limiting reproducibility and empirical validation. Overall the experiments and documentation are strong for a dataset paper but miss a few important analyses and release details that would make the contribution fully comprehensive.

main_claims: Proposes a procedure to build a Polish evaluation dataset for validating compositional distributional semantics (CDS) models comparable to the SICK corpus.
Creates a Polish test corpus of 10K sentence pairs (20K ordered pairs including reverses) annotated for semantic relatedness and bidirectional entailment.

Introduces language-specific sentence transformation rules for Polish, automatic transformation on dependency structures plus human verification, and a two-stage annotation workflow to ensure data quality.
_summary.methodology: Adapted the SICK-building steps to Polish by selecting image-based seed sentences (from ImageFlickr), applying automatic, dependency-structure-based language-specific transformation rules, assembling sentence pairs according to a defined pairing scheme (including reversed pairs), and using a two-stage human annotation process with leader verification and post-corrections.
datasets: Polish test corpus (10K sentence pairs, derived from 1K images)
SemEval-2012 Semantic Textual Similarity dataset (Agirre et al., 2012)
metrics: Fleiss' kappa (inter-annotator agreement on entailment, κ = 0.732)
Krippendorff's alpha (inter-annotator agreement on relatedness scores, α = 0.78)
limitations: Lack of necessary extraneous resources for Polish compared to English.
Transformation process is language-specific and relies on imperfect dependency parsing, causing errors in automatically transformed sentences that require human correction.
Entailment must be annotated bidirectionally because entailment is not symmetric (necessitating annotation overhead).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - compositional distributional semantics; dataset creation/evaluation

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in formal semantics and representation learning with many years publishing on compositionality theory and principled model evaluation.
traits: Downgrades papers that primarily present datasets or empirical pipelines without a clear theoretical contribution or formal evaluation framework (will deduct points if no formalization of target phenomenon is given).
Penalises papers that do not demonstrate downstream utility via at least two extrinsic tasks or ablation studies showing how the dataset changes model behavior (requires >=2 downstream evaluations).

Requires detailed annotation agreement statistics (expects reported inter-annotator agreement such as Krippendorff's alpha or Cohen's kappa ≥ 0.7) and will reduce score if only coarse agreement measures are given.

Downgrades reliance on automatic transformation rules unless accompanied by a thorough error analysis quantifying transformation failure modes (expects per-rule precision/recall or sampled error rates).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Very critical of empirical-only papers: focuses on principled definitions, rigorous evaluation, and demonstration of how the resource advances understanding of compositional semantics.

reviewer_b.name: Resource Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher with extensive experience building and using multilingual datasets and benchmarks for semantics and downstream NLP tasks.
traits: Rewards well-documented, language-specific datasets that enable community progress (gives higher scores when a new language resource is large-scale, has documented annotation workflow, and will be released publicly).

Excited by practical solutions: positively biases towards papers that adapt existing validated procedures (e.g., SICK construction) to new languages and include automation plus human verification rather than fully manual pipelines.
Values thoughtful annotation protocols and verification (expects a staged annotation process and leader verification; will upscore when such QA is present even if downstream evaluations are limited).
Prefers resources that provide balanced test sets (expects ~10k+ examples for release-scale benchmarks) and will reward scale and reproducibility over purely theoretical framing.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and supportive of new resources: emphasizes utility, reproducibility, and whether the dataset will be useful to the community, even if the paper is light on formal theory.
